preFix.title: Making a timber garden raised planter
preFix.subtitle: Build a simple box planter from treated softwood boards
preFix.excerpt: How to build a rectangular raised planter from tanalised softwood boards: cutting and squaring the frame, driving screws with countersunk holes for a flush finish, and lining the box before filling with compost.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: softwood lasts for many years without painting and needs no more skill than straight sawing and basic drilling. A planter 1200 mm long, 600 mm wide, and 350 mm tall is a practical starting size: large enough to grow vegetables or perennials, small enough to fill and move in sections if necessary.

Tanalised softwood boards, 150 mm x 22 mm, enough for two layers on all four sides at the chosen plan size. Four 75 x 75 mm corner posts cut to the full internal depth of the box. Exterior-grade 65 mm screws. A crosscut saw, cordless drill fitted with a countersink bit, tape measure, combination square, and carpenter's pencil. Butyl rubber or breathable landscape membrane to line the box.
Step 1, cut the boards and posts

Mark each cut with the combination square and carpenter's pencil, then crosscut the boards to length. Cut the four corner posts so they equal the full internal depth of the planter: these sit inside each corner and the side boards butt against their faces. Label all pieces before assembly to avoid confusion.
Stand two corner posts upright on a flat surface. Hold the first long board against the outside face of both posts and drill two

pilot holes through the board into the post, then drive 65 mm screws. Repeat for all four sides, adding the second board layer on top of the first. After fitting the first layer, measure both diagonals across the box: equal diagonals confirm the frame is square.
Step 3, add base boards, drainage, and liner

Fix base boards across the bottom of the frame with a 10 mm gap between each board for drainage, or drill 20 mm drainage holes at 300 mm centres across a solid base. Cut the liner to fit the inside of the box, fold it into the corners, and staple or tack it over the top edge of the upper boards. The liner keeps compost in at the joints while the gaps allow waterlogging to drain away.
What it looks like when it is right

All screw heads are flush with the board face. The corners are square and the frame does not rack when pushed from side to side. The liner sits smoothly against all four walls without pulling away at the corners. Filled with compost, the boards flex slightly under the load; this is normal in a single-skin board box and does not affect the structure or longevity.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
